preFix.title: Formal italic ligatures
preFix.subtitle: The ct, st, fi, fl and ff joins that belong in a disciplined chancery italic hand
preFix.excerpt: A ligature in formal italic joins two letters with a continuous pen stroke, reducing the number of lifts and giving the word a tighter visual unit. The ct, st, fi, fl, and ff ligatures are standard in the Renaissance chancery tradition. Each has a specific join point and a specific direction of departure, learning them correctly distinguishes formal italic from casual joining.
preFix.sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906), chapter on italic hands and joins. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. The ct, st, fi, fl ligatures are canonical in the chancery italic tradition.

content.text: in formal italic joins letters without a pen lift at a specific, repeatable point. The five standard ligatures, ct, st, fi, fl, ff, are defined by their join point, not by a freehand decision about where the connection feels natural. Learning the correct join for each one prevents the habits that make italic ligatures look casual or inconsistent. The
from the first letter becomes the entry stroke of the second.

items.name: Light box, A4 (optional for tracing practice sheets)

content.text: Rule a practice sheet at Italic proportions: 5 nib widths x-height, ascenders 4 nib widths above waist, descenders 3 below baseline. Nib angle 45°. Slope 5-10° from vertical.
Each description names the join point and the direction of the connecting stroke.

The c exits with a hairline rise from the mid-waist. The pen travels directly into the crossbar of the t, crossing the t-stem at x-height midpoint. The crossbar of the t and the exit of the c are one stroke: the pen goes across, then drops into the t-stem.

The s exits at the waist with a short curved exit. This exit connects directly to the top of the t-stem as a lead-in. The t crossbar is then drawn separately. The join sits at the very top of the letter body, just at the waist line.
The f descends to the baseline exit with a rising exit stroke at the

midpoint. The entry of the i begins at the waist from this rising exit. The f-dot and i-dot are placed after completing both letters. The fi dot in many formal italic traditions is a single shared dot positioned over the junction.

Same entry as fi, but the exit of the f connects to the top of the l ascender. The join works because the f-crossbar and the l-top are at the same height. Draw the full f body, let the exit rise to meet the l ascender start.

The double f shares one crossbar for both letters. Draw the first f descender and body; continue the crossbar across both f stems before descending the second f. The shared crossbar is the diagnostic of the ff ligature, two separate crossbars indicate unjoined letters, not a ligature.

items.fix: The c exit must rise to exactly x-height midpoint before turning horizontal. If it exits lower, it becomes a lead-in rather than a crossbar.
items.cause: The exit of the c is not meeting the t-crossbar height.
items.symptom: The ct join looks like a disconnected hairline rather than a crossbar

items.fix: The connecting stroke must curve, not straighten. Think of it as the natural exit arc of the f meeting the natural entry arc of the i at the waist level.
items.cause: The join stroke is too vertical, reading as a separate stroke rather than a flowing connection.
items.symptom: The fi ligature looks awkward, the f and i are identifiably separate

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
